findings.title: Discriminator collision — type confusion via duplicate 8-byte instruction discriminator
findings.description: Multiple instructions in the candy-machine program use the same 8-byte Anchor discriminator. Anchor derives instruction discriminators from the first 8 bytes of the SHA256 hash of the function name. When two or more public functions share the same discriminator, the runtime dispatches to whichever handler appears first in the dispatch table. An attacker who can craft a transaction with the colliding discriminator will trigger the wrong instruction, bypassing intended access controls.
findings.impact: Complete execution context confusion. An attacker can trigger unintended instruction handlers — including admin operations, minting, or configuration changes — by submitting a transaction with the colliding discriminator. This allows unauthorized minting, configuration hijacking, or fund drainage depending on which instruction collides with which.
findings.recommendation: Audit all public function names in the program for hash collisions using Anchor's IDL tooling or a custom script that enumerates discriminators. Rename colliding functions to unique names. Add a comprehensive test suite that validates each instruction discriminator is unique.
findings.root_cause: Two or more public functions in the candy-machine program share the same 8-byte Anchor discriminator. Anchor generates discriminators from the SHA256 hash of the lowercase function name; collisions cause the runtime to dispatch to an unintended handler based on dispatch-table ordering.

findings.title: Manual init without Anchor discriminator — reinit attack possible
findings.description: The init_machine instruction manually sets CandyMachine fields without writing the 8-byte Anchor discriminator. Anchor's #[account] macro normally writes an 8-byte discriminator on init and verifies it on load. Since init_machine bypasses this mechanism by assigning fields directly on an uninitialized Account, no discriminator is stored. An attacker who obtains control of the account (e.g., a previously-used PDA whose lamports were refunded) can call init_machine again and reset the machine's state.
findings.impact: Reinitialization of candy machine state. An attacker who acquires a closed account's key can reinitialize the machine, resetting items_available and items_redeemed to arbitrary values. This allows minting beyond intended supply limits or hijacking the machine's configuration.
findings.recommendation: Use Anchor's #[account(init, ...)] constraint in the InitMachine struct to trigger automatic discriminator writing. Alternatively, manually call Discriminator::write_account(&mut ctx.accounts.machine.to_account_info()) before field assignment. Remove the manual field assignment pattern entirely.
findings.root_cause: init_machine assigns account fields without calling Anchor's discriminator write. The #[account] type is used in the struct definition but the instruction bypasses Anchor's Account<T> wrapper that would normally enforce discriminator semantics.

findings.title: Account constraints bypassed — anyone can change mint authority
findings.description: The set_authority instruction accepts mint_account as AccountInfo and assigns mint_account.authority = new_authority directly. No has_one constraint ties the current authority to the stored key, no is_signer check verifies the current authority signed, and no realloc constraints enforce mutability expectations. Any account can be passed as mint_account, and any authority value can be set as the new authority.
findings.impact: Unauthorized mint authority transfer. An attacker can redirect the mint authority of any candy machine's associated mint account to a key they control, enabling them to mint arbitrary tokens or freeze the collection.
findings.recommendation: Add #[account(has_one = authority)] on mint_account and change authority: AccountInfo to authority: Signer. Anchor's constraint system will verify the signing authority matches the stored authority before allowing the update. Alternatively, derive the mint authority from a PDA seeded on the machine's stored authority.
findings.root_cause: The SetAuth struct declares mint_account and authority as plain AccountInfo with no #[account] wrapper and no has_one constraint. The instruction assigns to .authority via raw AccountInfo mutation without Anchor's constraint validation layer.

findings.title: Missing signer on admin config update — anyone can modify price
findings.description: The update_config instruction accepts authority as AccountInfo instead of Signer and performs no is_signer check. Any caller can pass any account as authority and update the config price to any value. The ConfigData account is marked mutable but the authority field is not constrained to require a signature.
findings.impact: Unauthorized price manipulation. An attacker can set the candy machine price to 0 (free minting) or to an extreme value that prevents legitimate purchases, disrupting the sale mechanism or enabling free extraction of limited items.
findings.recommendation: Change authority: AccountInfo to authority: Signer in the UpdateConfig struct. Anchor will enforce that the authority must sign the transaction. Add a has_one constraint: #[account(has_one = authority, mut)] on the config field to bind the config account to the stored authority.
findings.root_cause: The authority field was declared as AccountInfo instead of Signer. Anchor's deserializer accepts any AccountInfo regardless of whether it signed the transaction. No is_signer gate exists in the instruction body.

findings.title: Arithmetic overflow in item count — supply limit bypass
findings.description: The add_items instruction computes total by iterating over counts and summing with the default + operator on u32. In Solana's release mode, this wraps silently on overflow. If counts contains values whose sum exceeds u32::MAX (4,294,967,295), total wraps to a small value. The machine's items_available is set to this wrapped value, allowing minting beyond the intended supply.
findings.impact: Integer overflow corrupts the supply counter. A wrapped items_available value can be set to 0 or a small number, enabling either unlimited minting or premature sale closure. This breaks the core scarcity mechanism of the candy machine.
findings.recommendation: Use checked_add in the loop: `total = total.checked_add(c).ok_or(ErrorCode::Overflow)?;`. Add an Overflow variant to the program's error enum. Replace all arithmetic on user-controlled amounts with checked operations.
findings.root_cause: The add_items loop used default wrapping arithmetic (total + c) on u32 instead of checked_add. In Solana release mode, wrapping on overflow is silent and corrupts the accounting state.

findings.title: Reentrancy via mint callback — mint count can be incremented multiple times
findings.description: The mint_with_callback instruction performs token::mint_to via CPI and then increments machine.items_redeemed. No reentrancy guard exists between the CPI call and the state update. If the token program or any callback invoked during the CPI re-enters mint_with_callback, items_redeemed will be incremented multiple times for a single actual mint operation. This breaks the 1:1 mapping between mints and redemptions.
findings.impact: Double-counting of mint redemptions. A re-entrant attacker can increment items_redeemed multiple times per actual mint, causing the machine to report fewer remaining items than actually exist. This enables minting more NFTs than the configured supply.
findings.recommendation: Move all state updates before CPI calls: store `items_redeemed + 1` to a local variable first, perform the CPI, then write the incremented value. Alternatively, use a dedicated ReentrancyGuard account with a check-and-set pattern: require!(guard.is_initialized == false); guard.is_initialized = true; /* do CPI */; guard.is_initialized = false;.
findings.root_cause: The external call (token::mint_to CPI) precedes the state mutation (items_redeemed += 1). Without a reentrancy guard, the instruction is vulnerable to a call-depth attack where the CPI triggers a callback that re-enters the same instruction.

findings.title: Token-2022 transfer fee not accounted — recipient receives less than sent
findings.description: The transfer_nft instruction calls token::transfer with the full amount parameter. Token-2022 introduces transfer fees that are deducted from the transferred amount before the recipient receives funds. By sending the nominal amount, the recipient receives amount - fee_amount, not the intended amount. The deficit is burned or retained by the fee authority depending on the token configuration.
findings.impact: Fund loss for NFT recipients. When transferring Token-2022 tokens with fees, the recipient receives less than the sender specified. Over repeated transfers, the cumulative fee leakage can be significant. Users may believe they transferred the full amount while recipients experience partial receipt.
findings.recommendation: Query the token's transfer fee configuration via the Token-2022 extension. Calculate the maximum fee for the given amount before calling transfer, and verify the recipient's balance increase matches the expected net amount. Use transfer_checked instead of transfer to validate fee withheld amounts.
findings.root_cause: The instruction used token::transfer without accounting for Token-2022 transfer fees. The nominal amount is transferred, but the fee is deducted before the recipient balance updates, causing a discrepancy.

findings.title: Non-transferable token bypass via hook invocation — unauthorized transfer
findings.description: The force_transfer instruction uses Program<'info, Token2022> to invoke the token program's transfer instruction, bypassing the non-transferable extension's owner-invariant check. The non-transferable extension enforces that tokens with this extension cannot be transferred unless the transfer is initiated by the token owner via the standard path. By routing through the raw program instruction, the extension's transfer hook is never called.
findings.impact: Unauthorized transfer of non-transferable tokens. NFTs marked as non-transferable can be stolen from their owners by anyone who can get a transaction through this program. This defeats the entire purpose of the non-transferable extension.
findings.recommendation: Remove force_transfer entirely — it is a privilege-escalation vector. If a legitimate force-transfer use case exists (e.g., admin override for lost keys), implement it with proper authorization checks: require the caller is the stored authority, and emit a ForceTransfer event for auditability.
findings.root_cause: force_transfer bypasses Anchor's account constraints and invokes the Token2022 program directly via Program<'info, Token2022>, routing around the non-transferable extension's security hooks.

findings.title: init_if_needed without one-time bump enforcement — collection can be reinitialized
findings.description: The create_collection instruction accepts a bump parameter but does not enforce one-time use. Without storing and checking the bump against a previously-used value, the instruction can be called multiple times with the same or different bump values to reinitialize the Collection account. Each reinitialization resets the authority and size fields, allowing an attacker to hijack the collection or corrupt its state.
findings.impact: Collection reinitialization. An attacker who controls the collection account key can reinitialize it, changing the authority to any key they control or resetting the collection size to inflate apparent supply.
findings.recommendation: Store the bump in the Collection account: pub bump: u8. On the first call, set authority, size, and bump. On subsequent calls, verify the existing bump matches the provided bump: require!(existing_bump == bump, CollectionError::AlreadyInitialized). Use Anchor's init_if_needed pattern with a bump constraint: #[account(init_if_needed, payer = authority, bump, space = 64)].
findings.root_cause: create_collection accepts a bump parameter but never stores it or verifies it matches a previously-stored value. The instruction is idempotent in behavior but not in implementation — multiple calls reset account state.

findings.title: Duplicate mutable account in batch mint — single account mutated multiple times
findings.description: The batch_mint instruction uses the same mint AccountInfo for multiple operations within a single instruction execution. While Solana generally permits one account to be the target of multiple mutations in a single transaction, the sequencing of these mutations is not explicitly controlled. Additionally, the total computation uses unchecked addition which could overflow.
findings.impact: Potential state inconsistency between intended and actual mint totals. If the account's state changes between sequential operations, or if the total overflows, the mint operation may not reflect the intended amount. The mint account could end up with a different supply than expected.
findings.recommendation: Use a single checked mint_to call with the precomputed total: `let total = amounts.iter().try_fold(0u64, |acc, &a| acc.checked_add(a)).ok_or(ErrorCode::Overflow)?; token::mint_to(ctx.accounts.mint_ctx(), total)?;`. Remove the sequential pattern that iterates multiple times.
findings.root_cause: The batch_mint instruction processes amounts in a loop and performs a single mint_to with the total, but the account is referenced multiple times during the instruction execution without explicit sequencing guarantees from Anchor's constraint system.

findings.title: Token-2022 transfer hook CPI routing — extra accounts not verified
findings.description: The create_metadata instruction builds an Instruction using ctx.accounts.token_program as the program_id and passes ctx.remaining_accounts directly to the invoke call. For Token-2022 programs with transfer hook extensions, additional mandatory accounts must be included in the instruction. By not validating the contents of remaining_accounts against the expected transfer hook accounts, the instruction can fail silently or route to an unintended program with a mismatched account list.
findings.impact: CPI routing to token program with incomplete or mismatched account list. If the token program has a transfer hook extension, the missing extra accounts will cause the CPI to fail. An attacker could exploit this by crafting remaining_accounts to redirect to a different program that accepts the provided account list.
findings.recommendation: Validate remaining_accounts against the token program's expected extra accounts before passing them to invoke. Query the transfer hook extension's required accounts from the mint metadata. If the program is Token-2022, extract the transfer hook instruction and its required accounts from the remaining_accounts.
findings.root_cause: ctx.remaining_accounts was forwarded directly to invoke without validating that the accounts match the expected extra accounts for the target program's instruction. Any account list can be passed through.

findings.title: Unsafe deserialization without owner check — arbitrary data deserialized as metadata
findings.description: The read_metadata instruction borrows account data and calls Metadata::try_from_slice(&data[8..]) without verifying the account owner is the metadata program. Any account holding 8 bytes of data can be passed to this instruction, and the slice will be deserialized as Metadata regardless of whether it was created by the metadata program. This enables type confusion attacks where arbitrary account data is interpreted as structured metadata.
findings.impact: Type confusion via arbitrary deserialization. An attacker can pass any account with sufficient data and the first 8 bytes will be skipped (treated as a discriminator), then the remaining bytes will be interpreted as Metadata fields. This could leak sensitive account data or enable further exploitation if the deserialized values are used in subsequent logic.
findings.recommendation: Add an owner check before deserialization: `require!(ctx.accounts.metadata_account.owner == &program_id, MetadataError::InvalidOwner);`. Use Anchor's Account<'info, Metadata> wrapper instead of raw try_borrow_data, which enforces owner validation automatically.
findings.root_cause: The instruction used try_borrow_data on an AccountInfo without verifying the account owner matches the metadata program. Anchor's Account<T> wrapper enforces owner validation; raw AccountInfo access bypasses this protection.

findings.title: Arbitrary CPI via remaining_accounts in metadata update — full program hijack
findings.description: The update_metadata instruction accepts target_program: AccountInfo and constructs an Instruction with that program_id, an empty account list, and new_uri bytes as data. It then passes ctx.remaining_accounts directly to invoke. An attacker can specify any program (including System Program, Token Program, or a malicious program) as target_program, provide any instruction data via new_uri, and supply arbitrary accounts via remaining_accounts. This enables arbitrary CPI to any program the caller has authority over.
findings.impact: Full arbitrary CPI execution. An attacker can invoke any program with attacker-controlled instruction data and accounts. This can be used to transfer tokens, close accounts, modify program state, or escalate privileges by exploiting the update_metadata instruction's signing context.
findings.recommendation: Remove the arbitrary CPI capability entirely. Replace with a dedicated metadata update instruction that only modifies the metadata account's uri field directly, without invoking external programs. If a plugin architecture is genuinely needed, maintain an allowlist of approved program IDs in a config account and validate target_program against the allowlist.
findings.root_cause: target_program was accepted as a user-supplied AccountInfo without validation against an allowlist. remaining_accounts was forwarded directly to invoke without filtering, enabling arbitrary CPI to any program.

findings.title: Missing writable enforcement — account mutated without writable constraint
findings.description: The SetUpdateAuth struct declares metadata_account with #[account(mut)] but does not verify the account is writable in the Solana runtime sense. The metadata_account field is mutated in the instruction body (update_authority field assignment), but the AccountMeta used in any subsequent CPI would not mark it as writable (writable=false by default). While Anchor's mut constraint does set writable=true in generated CPIs, the account is not verified to be genuinely writable at the constraint level.
findings.impact: Failed instruction execution. If the account is not actually writable (e.g., a system account or an account passed without the writable flag in a raw instruction), the mutation will fail at runtime. This creates a confusing error path and potential for denial of service if the instruction is called with a non-writable account.
findings.recommendation: Verify the account is genuinely writable by checking account.is_writable in the instruction body before performing the mutation. Add a custom validation check: `if !ctx.accounts.metadata_account.is_writable { return Err(ProgramError::InvalidArgument.into()); }`. Anchor's #[account(mut)] already sets writable=true in AccountMeta; add an explicit assertion for defense-in-depth.
findings.root_cause: The metadata_account was declared with #[account(mut)] but the SetUpdateAuth struct does not include a Signer constraint on authority, and the instruction mutates the account without verifying runtime writability. Anchor's mut constraint generates writable=true in AccountMeta for CPIs, but the account could be passed without the writable flag in a raw instruction path.
